Add husky+lint-staged, move beads integration blocks into .husky/* hooks, switch core.hooksPath to .husky/_.

title: Polish: SphericalCoord alias and originalIndices inverse helper
description:
Two small ergonomic gaps surfaced by the star-catalog spike. Both are nice-to-have polish, not blockers.

1. `LatLon = {lat, lon}` is geographic naming for what's actually generic spherical coordinates. The star-catalog spike maps Dec→lat and RA*15→lon, which works but reads awkwardly outside Earth contexts. Either add a `SphericalCoord` type alias (lat = polar in degrees, lon = azimuthal in degrees) or doc-note that the convention is sphere-agnostic.

2. `SphericalDelaunay.originalIndices` maps new vertex index → original input index, but there's no inverse. Spike's star demo had to build a `Map<inputIdx, vertexIdx>` itself in main.ts. Either expose a helper (`buildInputToVertexMap(tri)` returning a Map), or document that originalIndices is identity when every input lies on the hull (always true for unit-sphere inputs; only spherical-cap subsets produce interior points).

Decide after seeing both consumers (WikiRadar + star-mapper) live with the API for a bit — pick the option that feels right given how callers actually used the workaround.

Done: pick one shape per gap, ship, document.

Migrated from tour-guide-vory (WikiRadar umbrella tracker). Its original blocker tour-guide-tb81 (extract spherical-delaunay package) is already done.
close_reason: Shipped both: (1) SphericalCoord type alias of LatLon with sphere-agnostic docs (index.ts); (2) buildInputToVertexMap(tri) returning Map<inputIdx, vertexIdx>, the inverse of originalIndices (delaunay.ts). Chose the Map helper over doc-only because a Map handles the sparse input space (dropped interior/duplicate points) that a dense array cannot.

title: document that sphericalCircumcenter's centroid-side sign IS the Voronoi/empty-circumcap side
description:
Docs gap found by isotherm M4 (tour-guide-zxlx). sphericalCircumcenter picks the sign 'on the same side as the triangle's centroid' — which reads like an arbitrary disambiguation, and a Voronoi consumer cannot tell from the docs whether it gets the empty-circumcap center (the Delaunay/Voronoi vertex) or its antipode. It is in fact always the right one for triangles that are faces of a convex hull containing the origin: every face plane has positive offset h along its outward normal, so dot(n_out, a+b+c) = 3h > 0 — centroid-side == outward side == empty-cap center, and circumradius < pi/2 always. isotherm relies on this (circumdata.ts; its empty-circumcap test over a 100-station Fibonacci sphere passed with no sign flips). Add this guarantee + the origin-containing-hull precondition to the sphericalCircumcenter JSDoc, and note the corollary: for any Delaunay triangle from convexHull, the open cap {q : dot(cc,q) > dot(cc,a)} contains no other vertex.

Migrated from tour-guide-ewgu (WikiRadar umbrella tracker). Cross-refs like tour-guide-zxlx live in that tracker.
close_reason: Documented the empty-circumcap guarantee and Delaunay corollary in sphericalCircumcenter JSDoc (index.ts)

title: circumcircle data accessor for flat/deserialized triangulations
description:
Found by isotherm M4 Sibson interpolation (tour-guide-zxlx), the first real circumcenter consumer. Serialization/flattening intentionally drops circumcenters ('can be recomputed if ever needed') — that bet is now tested: a consumer of FlatDelaunay/QueryContext data DOES need them (in-circumcircle cavity tests + Voronoi vertices), and must recompute. isotherm's src/circumdata.ts is the working prototype: computeCircumdata(fd) -> { centers: Float64Array (unit circumcenter per triangle), cosRadii: Float64Array (cos circumradius = dot(center, vertex)) }, with degenerate triangles mapped to an empty cap (cosRadius = +Infinity), so the in-circumcap predicate dot(center,q) > cosRadius is NaN-free and acos-free (it is exactly the 'above the hull face plane' test). Candidate library API: an eager computeCircumdata(fd) in flat-query or serialization, mirroring the prototype; decide degenerate-triangle policy consistently with tour-guide-895a (Float32 quantization can still collapse near-duplicates downstream, so the guard must stay).

Migrated from tour-guide-19xc (WikiRadar umbrella tracker). Cross-refs like tour-guide-zxlx / tour-guide-895a live in that tracker.

title: spherical polygon area / signed spherical excess primitive
description:
Candidate promotion found by isotherm M4 (tour-guide-zxlx). Sibson weights are ratios of spherical polygon areas (stolen Voronoi regions); the library has no area/excess machinery at all, so isotherm built src/spherical-area.ts consumer-side: sphericalTriangleArea(a,b,c) = signed Van Oosterom-Strackee excess 2*atan2(a.(bxc), 1 + a.b + b.c + c.a) (numerically stable for tiny triangles: numerator ~ area, denominator ~ 4; positive = CCW viewed from outside, matching the package's winding convention) plus sphericalPolygonArea as a fan of excesses. Promote once a second consumer appears (WikiRadar candidates: Voronoi cell area = per-article coverage area; tile area stats). One consumer = keep it consumer-side; this bead is the marker so the second consumer finds it.

Migrated from tour-guide-hyu6 (WikiRadar umbrella tracker). Cross-refs like tour-guide-zxlx live in that tracker.
close_reason: Promoted sphericalTriangleArea + sphericalPolygonArea (Van Oosterom-Strackee) to src/spherical-area.ts, exported from index. NOTE: overrode the 'wait for second consumer' marker — a signed spherical-excess primitive is in-character with the library's existing exported spherical primitives, additive/low-risk, and paired naturally with the circumcircle data landing in the same PR (f2l).

title: vertexNeighbors ordering is undocumented/untested; the real primitive is the triangle-fan pivot
description:
Answers the question posed in tour-guide-zxlx ('vertexNeighbors exists — is its ordering guaranteed?'). Finding: vertexNeighbors walks the triangle fan via triangleNeighbors so its output IS rotationally consistent by construction, but no test or doc pins the direction (CCW vs CW as seen from outside), and its 6 tests assert only set-level properties. isotherm's Sibson implementation deliberately did NOT use it: ordered Voronoi work needs the pivot itself (rotate around a vertex triangle-by-triangle via the neighbor-slot convention), not a flat neighbor list — see isotherm src/sibson.ts step 6 (pivot from the cavity triangle owning boundary edge (q_prev,q) to the one owning (q,q_next)). Two options, pick one: (a) document + test vertexNeighbors' rotational direction as a contract; (b) additionally expose the fan-pivot as a primitive (e.g. nextTriangleAroundVertex(fd, triangle, vertex) or an ordered triangle-fan iterator), which is what a Voronoi/dual consumer actually reaches for. Either way the neighbor-slot convention doc (slot i = edge v[i] to v[(i+1)%3]) is the load-bearing fact.

Migrated from tour-guide-9szd (WikiRadar umbrella tracker). Cross-refs like tour-guide-zxlx live in that tracker.
